protein_name: a transcriptional activator and acetyltransferase that forms a heterodimeric complex with ARNTL. A key component of the circadian clock oscillator, which regulates a wide array of physiological functions including metabolism, sleep, body temperature, blood pressure, endocrine, immune, cardiovascular, and renal function. The circadian clock consists of two major components: the central clock, residing in the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the brain, and the peripheral clocks that are present in nearly every tissue and organ system. The oscillator includes the CRY proteins, CLOCK or NPAS2, ARNTL or ARNTL2, CSNK1D and/or CSNK1E, TIMELESS and the PER proteins. Efficient DNA binding requires dimerization with another bHLH protein. Heterodimerization with ARNTL is required for E-box-dependent transactivation, for CLOCK nuclear translocation and degradation, and, for phosphorylation of both CLOCK and ARNTL. CLOCK-ARNTL heterodimers activate E-box element (3'- CACGTG-5') transcription of a number of proteins of the circadian clock including PER1 and PER2. CLOCK specifically binds to the half-site 5'-CAC-3', while ARNTL binds to the half-site 5'-GTGA-3'. Interaction of the CLOCK-ARNTL heterodimer with PER or CRY inhibits transcription activation. Possesses intrinsic acetyltransferase activity that enables the remodeling of circadian chromatin by acetylating histones and nonhistone proteins, including its own partner ARNTL. Its acetyltransferase activity is as important as its transcription activity in circadian control. Represses glucocorticoid receptor transcriptional activity by acetylating GR in its hinge region. Acetylates metabolic enzymes IMPDH2 and NDUFA9 in a circadian manner. Facilitated by BMAL1, rhythmically interacts with and acetylates argininosuccinate synthase 1 (ASS1) leading to enzymatic inhibition of ASS1 as well as the circadian oscillation of arginine biosynthesis and subsequent ureagenesis. Plays a role in DNA damage response (DDR) signaling during the S phase. Expressed in all tissues examined. Highest levels in testis and skeletal muscle. Low levels in thymus, lung and liver. Expressed in all brain regions with highest levels in cerebellum. Highly expressed in the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN). Note: This description may include information from UniProtKB.
